title: Coral Bleaching and Reef Recovery
passage:
Coral reefs owe their vivid colors, and much of their nourishment, to microscopic algae that live within the tissues of the coral polyps. The two organisms exist in a symbiosis: the algae photosynthesize and pass a large share of the resulting nutrients to their hosts, while the coral provides the algae with shelter and the raw materials of photosynthesis. When corals are subjected to stress—most commonly a sustained rise in water temperature—they expel these algae. Deprived of both their pigment and their principal food source, the corals turn a stark white, a phenomenon known as bleaching.

Bleaching is not the same as death. A bleached coral is weakened and may starve if the stressful conditions persist, but if temperatures subside before the polyps die, algae can recolonize the tissues and the coral may regain its color and resume normal growth. Whether a given reef recovers therefore depends heavily on how long the stress lasts and how severe it is.

Recovery also varies with the corals themselves. Researchers have observed that some species reliably regain their algae and rebound after a bleaching episode, while others, exposed to the same conditions, recover slowly or not at all. The outcome of bleaching, in short, is not uniform: it reflects both the duration of the stress and the particular corals that endure it.

title: Medieval Craft Guilds
passage:
In the towns of medieval Europe, the production and sale of most manufactured goods were organized around craft guilds—associations of artisans practicing a common trade. A guild was far more than a club of like-minded workers; it was an institution that regulated nearly every aspect of its craft within a town.

Foremost among a guild's functions was the maintenance of quality. A guild set standards that the goods produced by its members had to meet, and officers inspected workshops and wares to enforce them. Because buyers could rarely judge the soundness of a finished article at a glance, this assurance of quality protected both the customer and the reputation of the trade. Guilds also regulated the training of new artisans. A young person entered a trade as an apprentice, working for years under a master before advancing; only after this period, and often the production of an accepted piece of work, could one attain the full status of a master and open a workshop.

Guilds served social ends as well as economic ones. Members paid dues into a common fund, and a guild commonly used these resources to support members who fell ill, and to aid the families of members who died or suffered hardship. In this way the guild knit its members into a community bound by mutual obligation, not merely shared occupation.

title: The Birth of Radio Astronomy
passage:
For most of its history, astronomy depended on visible light: whatever could be learned about the heavens was gathered by eye or, later, through optical telescopes. That limitation began to fall away because of an observation that no astronomer had set out to make. In the early 1930s, an engineer studying the sources of static that interfered with transatlantic radio communication noticed a faint hiss that recurred on a daily cycle. After ruling out terrestrial and solar sources, he concluded that the radio waves were coming from beyond the solar system, from the direction of the center of our galaxy.

The finding revealed that celestial objects emit not only visible light but also radio waves, and that these waves reach the ground. Astronomers could now build instruments to collect and study them, and the field of radio astronomy was born. Because radio waves penetrate clouds of gas and dust that block visible light, radio telescopes can reveal objects and regions of the universe that optical instruments cannot readily show.

What began as an effort to eliminate unwanted noise thus opened an entirely new window on the cosmos—one that has since allowed astronomers to map structures and detect phenomena invisible to the eye.
